items.title: ACCIDENT TOMORROW: BANDHAN BANK ? GUIDANCE CUT It has done 1% RoA in Q1FY27 but has cut exit Q4FY27 RoA guidance to 1.2-1.4% (vs 1.6-1.8% earlier). Th...
items.description_html:
ACCIDENT TOMORROW: BANDHAN BANK ?

GUIDANCE CUT

It has done 1% RoA in Q1FY27 but has cut exit Q4FY27 RoA guidance to 1.2-1.4% (vs 1.6-1.8% earlier). The cut is primarily due to higher opex (tech spends, LOS etc) and stable NIM (vs improvement earlier).

*RoA guidance revised downwards*

- Management _*cut FY27 exit Q4FY27 RoA guidance from 1.6-1.8% to 1.2-1.4%*_ due to the challenging external environment like elevated funding cost, energy crisis due to West Asia war, unpredictable monsoons and higher tech costs.

- The ~40bps cut in guided RoA was attributed to NIM (which now looks stable) and ~10bps impact on opex. Opex is towards tech spends etc.

- Maintain growth guidance of 14% YoY for FY27

- *NIM outlook*: NIM of the bank was stable QoQ at 6.2% as against earlier expectation of NIM expansion, which the management attributed to elevated funding costs.

Brokerage Conf call Summary

items.title: *Anthem Bio Q1FY27- Weak quarter, defferment in shipments impacted growth* Revs came in at INR 4.2bn (down 23% YoY), in line with our estimate *Q1 shi...
items.description_html:
*Anthem Bio Q1FY27- Weak quarter, defferment in shipments impacted growth*

Revs came in at INR 4.2bn (down 23% YoY), in line with our estimate

*Q1 shipments got deffered, recovery expected in coming quarters keeping the growth outlook for FY27 intact*

CRDMO segment witness decline of 25% YoY at INR 3.4bn

Specialty ingredients reported INR 774mn down 12% YoY.

*EBITDA stood at INR 1.5bn (down 21% YoY); broadly in line with our est.*

OPM lower at 36% down 774bps QoQ and up 55bps YoY

GM came at 65%, up 778bps YoY but declined 31 bps QoQ

Tax rate came in lower at 17% YoY

PAT came in at INR 1.2bn; down 12% YoY. We est INR 1.02bn

EPS of INR 2.1/share for Q1FY27

Currently we have Buy rating on stock.

Call tomorrow at 11.30am

PL

Marvell Technology just reported another strong quarter.

-Revenue grew 27.6% YoY in Q2 CY2026.
-Free cash flow margin expanded to 20%, up from 11.3% a year ago.

Pretty good results. Marvell has been shifting away from low-margin storage chips to AI and data center networking solutions, one of the biggest business model pivots in the semiconductor industry over the past decade.

items.title: I always look for qualitative signals when I research and look deep into any company Things like 1) How frequently the company talks to the media 2) H...
items.description_html:
I always look for qualitative signals when I research and look deep into any company

Things like
1) How frequently the company talks to the media
2) How often to KMP trade in and out of the stock
3) Does the company spend on digital presence and PR
4) Does hiring growth match guidance for company growth ( Linkedin/Naukri )
5)Suppliers and vendors reputation of the company, credit policy , payment terms , sales rep aggression
6) Does the company keep doing webinars with PMS managers to justify the business
7) Are they ecosystem focused or product focused , to understand the bigger picture with data like and acquisition for further growth close to greenfield plots
8) Nature of Clients with tenure mapping

These are some of the things that we must look into other than the typical B/S and Cash flow analysis also , the business is a moving picture with many parts , to understand the whole picture we must broaden the vision

items.description_html:
BREAKING: President Trump is preparing to announce 10% tariffs on "dozens of countries" this week, per FT.

Details include:

1. These new tariffs come as President Trump's current 10% tariffs expire later this week

2. President Trump's advisors have reportedly warned him against doing so

3. Advisors are reportedly worried about risking the economic shocks of the original trade war ahead of the midterm elections

4. The Trump Admin is also working on other investigations that could grant it the legal authority to propose higher tariffs

More tariffs are on the way.

items.title: POlycab on Data Centre Opportunity in India (Q1FY27 Concall) "We have read reports where the estimation is 8 to 16 GW or 18 GW from the current 1.8 GW...
items.description_html:
POlycab on Data Centre Opportunity in India (Q1FY27 Concall)

"We have read reports where the estimation is 8 to 16 GW or 18 GW from the current 1.8 GW. So we estimate that 1 megawatt translates into around INR 3.5 crores worth of cables, with 50% to 60% being conventional and the balance being optical fibre. So a sizable chunk of demand can come from the data centre. It's just a matter of timing. Definitely, an INR 20,000 to 25,000 crores market is there, but that's over a period of maybe 6-8 years.

Through our distribution channel, we have supplied a majority of the conventional pieces in the existing installations. I think we have called out earlier publicly that in the data centres of Vodafone Idea in Mohali, Pune, and South India, we have supplied the cables."

no reco

items.title: Zero waste is no longer just about better bins, recycling or landfills. At the Road to Antalya forum in Türkiye, delegates framed it as climate actio...
items.description_html:
Zero waste is no longer just about better bins, recycling or landfills.

At the Road to Antalya forum in Türkiye, delegates framed it as climate action — a way to reduce waste at source, cut dependence on virgin resources and rethink production itself.

For India, this raises a difficult question: can zero waste fit with continued petrochemical expansion, ask Vishvaja Sambath & Chythenyen Devika Kulasekaran of @cfa_ind
https://www.downtoearth.org.in/waste/the-zero-waste-forum-and-the-road-to-cop31-at-antalya-can-india-embrace-zero-waste-as-climate-action

items.description_html:
Africa is the world’s biggest wildfire hotspot, but a new study suggests its fire season is getting shorter.

Researchers say rains are arriving later into the usual drying period, keeping soils and vegetation wetter for longer and reducing the land burned by fires.

items.title: How inflection reflects in financial return ratios of a business? Following is the example of KVB: 1. ROE expanded from 16% to 18% almost. 2. ROA went...
items.description_html:
How inflection reflects in financial return ratios of a business?

Following is the example of KVB:

1. ROE expanded from 16% to 18% almost.
2. ROA went from 1.63% to a run rate of 2.11%
3. Net interest margins have improved non stop over last 5 Qs.
4. Cost to income has fallen thereby reflecting operating leverage in the business.

These inflection lasts for 2-3 years before new opex has to be incurred by a business to grow further.

Just a small example on how infections occur.

Keep tracking improvement in return ratios over every Quarterly to semi annual and annual results

Disc: no recommendation to buy or sell.

items.title: As the "cockroach" ‌movement continues, let's look at the numbers behind students' anger. India's education-spend down from 2.9% in FY20 to 2.7% of G...
items.description_html:
As the "cockroach" ‌movement continues, let's look at the numbers behind students' anger.
India's education-spend down from 2.9% in FY20 to 2.7% of GDP in FY26 against 6% target set by National Education Policy 2020. It trails lower-middle-income country average of 3.4% & South Asia regional average of 2.9%.
Health spend at 1.8% in FY26, far lower than 2.5% of GDP target set at National Health Policy 2017.
India's per-capita public health spending is among the world's lowest — less than Bhutan & Sri Lanka & far behind other BRICS nations.
India ranks 130 out of 193 countries on the UN Human Development Index which combines health, education & income. India sits in the "medium human development" category with a value of 0.685. China (78th), Sri Lanka (89th) and Bhutan (125th) rank above India; Bangladesh is tied at 130th.
Ref: UNDP's Human Development Report 2025 ("A Matter of Choice: People and Possibilities in the Age of AI"), based on 2023 data.

items.title: The leveraged FCNR product is a well discussed, but possibly misunderstood a little. An individual that pays tax in his home country will have an issu...
items.description_html:
The leveraged FCNR product is a well discussed, but possibly misunderstood a little.

An individual that pays tax in his home country will have an issue. If you borrow at 5% and get an FCNR at 6% it seems like a spread of 1% that can be leveraged 10x to get 10%. But that's not how tax authorities look at individuals, usually. The income is subject to tax, but the interest outflow is usually not allowed to be netted out from that income - though the US might allow it.

There's a NIIT (Net Investment Income Tax) in the US that applies if your income after deducting interest is higher than about 250K USD or so - but if you're getting 16% it's a fairly small percentage of the extra that doesn't diminish effective returns by much (3.8% of the 16% is 0.6% or so)

There could be a withholding tax on interest received if you borrow/lend outside your country - that is, loan from a gift city bank. FCNR doesn't have a withholding tax on payouts. Even if it did, that doesn't increase the cost of your loan, it just prepays part of the taxes you will owe anyhow on the interest earned. {Edited this part for clarity}

Typical withholding taxes when you PAY interest are held back from the interest paid itself. So if you pay 5% and withholding tax is 10%, you pay 4.5% as interest and 0.5% to your government. So it's not an additional cost.

Given that 6% is attractive (and Indian FCNR goes from 6% to 7.1% also), it's possible that a lot of FCNR deposits simply come from unleveraged players. For those that want the leverage, the tax implication of the countries they are resident in becomes important - so the middle east is the simplest (since there's no tax). The US, singapore, UK and Australia (regions with high Indian NRI presence) will have to get some tax advice before they take leverage, especially if they're getting loans from Gift city or such.

items.title: RT SOM | Orden Monetario: El mecanismo que describe @michaelxpettis es contabilidad, no opinión — una cuenta de capital gestionada junto a un super...
items.description_html:
RT SOM | Orden Monetario
El mecanismo que describe @michaelxpettis es contabilidad, no opinión — una cuenta de capital gestionada junto a un superávit comercial fuerza el desequilibrio hacia algún otro lugar, y el socio comercial abierto lo absorbe quiera o no.

Donde el marco necesita matiz es en su replicabilidad: esto solo funciona si un país reúne todas las piezas de la misma mano a la vez, y la mayoría no las reúne.

Hace falta capturar el eslabón de mayor valor añadido de la cadena, no cualquier volumen exportador — el superávit de materia prima por sí solo no compra este tipo de palanca, algo que la literatura de Prebisch-Singer sobre deterioro de términos de intercambio ya demostró hace décadas.

Hace falta capacidad financiera o, llegado el caso, militar para imponer el arreglo, además de proponerlo.

Hace falta una cuenta de capital que el propio país controle, lejos de tener una que el mercado le discipline desde fuera.

Y debajo de todo eso, hace falta control sobre la moneda en la que se liquida todo el sistema — la variable que de verdad hace el trabajo en los dos casos que venimos siguiendo esta semana: Washington asfixiando los envíos físicos de dólares a Irak hasta forzar cumplimiento en cuestión de semanas, y el estancamiento de cuarenta años de Japón tras el Acuerdo Plaza, que forzó una apreciación del yen sobre un país que en ese momento tenía todas las demás piezas menos esa.

Lectura SOM: ser policymaker en lugar de policytaker es una postura que es difícil que un país pueda simplemente adoptar.

Es una mano de cartas completa — y la carta de la moneda es, casi siempre, la que decide si las otras tres importan en absoluto.


Michael Pettis: Jamieson Greer: "If America doesn’t develop and implement its own policies to protect its industry and its workers, we will end up being policy takers. So if we’re not policymakers, we end up being policytakers."

I think this is among the most important points Greer makes in

items.description_html:
Paytm Q1FY27: Strong set🟩

REV grew 28% YoY & 8% QoQ to 2,448 Cr
EBITDA grew 182% YoY & 54% QoQ to 203 Cr
PAT grew 79% YoY to 220 Cr

REV grew 28% YoY, but indirect expenses grew only 6% YoY

EBITDA margin improved to 8% vs 4% last year (Operating leverage in play🔥)

Indirect expenses as a % of REV reduced from 56% to 47%

On a comparable basis, excluding PIDF incentive, performance was even stronger

Merchant GMV grew 31% to 7.1 lakh Cr
Merchant subscriptions stood at 1.57 Cr, up 27 lakh YoY

Payment processing margin is now above 4 bps vs 3 bps earlier

Consumer payments are also gaining share
Customer UPI GTV grew 45% to 5.9 lakh Cr
MTU increased by 60 lakh YoY to 8 Cr

Paytm highlighted that consumer payments grew 2.2x industry growth, with Paytm growth at 45% vs industry UPI growth of 20%

Financial services is becoming the high margin growth engine

Distribution of financial services REV grew 45% YoY to 814 Cr

Key financial services customers increased 34% YoY to 7.6 lakh

More than 50% of merchant loan disbursements are to repeat borrowers

Merchant loans, Postpaid, personal loans, equity broking, MTF & wealth products are becoming monetisation layers

Paytm is using AI across merchant onboarding, fraud prevention, customer service, marketing stack, cross sell & collections

Current Cash balance of 13,529 Cr

Management commentary on margins was also strong

Paytm said it now has better visibility to achieve 15-20% EBITDA margin over the next 2-3 years so operating leverage will continue to play out in future

items.title: Why markets became more excited about Tatva Chintan? The biggest trigger was the Semiconductor Chemical update Tatva Chintan reported a strong Q1FY27 ...
items.description_html:
Why markets became more excited about Tatva Chintan?

The biggest trigger was the Semiconductor Chemical update

Tatva Chintan reported a strong Q1FY27
REV grew 43% YoY & 25% QoQ to 167 Cr
EBITDA grew 86% YoY & 15% QoQ to 32 Cr

The important point is that growth was largely volume led & not just pricing

They reiterated FY27 guidance of 25-30% REV growth with EBITDA margin in the 20-22% range

Growth is becoming more broad based across PTC, SDA, pharma & agro intermediates

PTC & SDA remain strong base businesses, while pharma / agro intermediates & electrolyte salts are starting to scale

The company delivered its first commercial plant scale batch to a customer, and the batch has been successfully qualified

This product is used as a key starting material for semiconductor manufacturing & also has applications in PCB etching & circuit cleaning

Management highlighted that the qualification was achieved for the most stringent application - as a key starting block for semiconductors

However, commercialization is not expected before Q4FY28, so its a long term optionality

Another important update was the 200 Cr greenfield capex

The board has approved a new multi-product, multi purpose greenfield manufacturing facility

Management expects asset turnover of 1.2-1.5x, which implies around 300 Cr peak REV potential

Management also mentioned that 7-8 products can move into commercial / pilot stage in the near future

On long term growth, management indicated that the company can grow at 20-25% CAGR over the next 3-4 years

Earlier investments in R&D, new chemistries, customer approvals & technology capabilities are now converting into commercial revenue

items.title: Jamieson Greer: "If America doesn’t develop and implement its own policies to protect its industry and its workers, we will end up being policy taker...
items.description_html:
Jamieson Greer: "If America doesn’t develop and implement its own policies to protect its industry and its workers, we will end up being policy takers. So if we’re not policymakers, we end up being policytakers."

I think this is among the most important points Greer makes in this New York Times interview, but it is not nearly as well-understood by policymakers around the world as it should be.

What Greer is arguing is that in a hyperglobalized world in which some economies exert much greater control over their external accounts, while others exert much less, if the former also implement aggressive domestic industrial policies, they will be able to externalize the resulting imbalances through their trade and capital accounts.

This has extremely important implications that mainstream economists almost alway miss. Not only are economies that exert much greater control over their external accounts able to determine their domestic imbalances, they are also able to determine the domestic imbalances of their more open trade partners through their control of both countries' external imbalances.

In effect the former are able to make their domestic industrial policies also the industrial polices, in reverse, of the latter.

For example, if a country that can control its external accounts decides that as a matter of domestic industrial policy it wants to shift out of producing services and/or commodities and into manufacturing, and so increase its share of global manufacturing, its more open trade partners will automatically reduce their share of global manufacturing as they shift either into services, like the EU, or into commodity production, like Brazil.

There will of course be constituencies in the EU that say that producing services is better than producing manufactured goods, and constituencies in Brazil that say that producing commodities is better than producing manufactured goods, and in some cases they may even be right.

But the point that they miss is that these shifts were not the result of local market conditions, nor of the decisions of their own policymakers. The shifts were imposed upon them by the policymakers of their more closed and more aggressive trade partners.

The changes in their economies were designed, in other words, not to accommodate their own economic needs but rather to accommodate the economic needs of their trade partners. It should be obvious (but isn't to many economists) that this is not how free markets work.
https://www.nytimes.com/2026/07/20/podcasts/the-daily/more-trump-tariffs-are-coming.html?smid=tw-share

items.description_html:
*MSCI Aug'26 Review: Initial Cut-off Assessment after Day 1 | Biocon at a crucial juncture*

_Nuvama Alternative & Quant Research_

• The MSCI August review cut-off window commenced yesterday, and we are reiterating our assessment based on Day 1 of the cut-off period.

• Historically, MSCI has tended to determine the cut-off within the initial few days of the 10-working-day observation window.

• Our current view remains unchanged >>
* Three high-conviction inclusions
* Three high-conviction exclusions

• Biocon continues to be the only stock on the borderline. Based purely on Day 1 price cut-off, the stock missed the inclusion threshold by approximately 2.4%.

• If Biocon trades comfortably above ₹447 today and Day 2 is eventually chosen by MSCI as the cut-off date, the stock has the potential to qualify for inclusion.

• The actual cut-off date used by MSCI will only be known on August 13, when the review announcement is released.

*Key Dates*
* Cut-off Window: July 20 – July 31
* Announcement: August 13 (3:30 AM IST)
* Effective Date: August 31

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: Patym - Strong Q1; but defers bonus plans GS on Paytm Buy, TP Rs 1500 Paytm saw an acceleration in revenue growth in 1QFY27 & reported...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
Patym - Strong Q1; but defers bonus plans

GS on Paytm
Buy, TP Rs 1500
Paytm saw an acceleration in revenue growth in 1QFY27 & reported a meaningful beat on EBITDA (+20% vs GSe)
Within payments, growth was driven by market share gains in both offline & online, with latter a function of ability to onboard new merchants starting late last year
Additionally, strong traction in merchant loan distribution has sustained, while scale up of consumer products such as postpaid could continue to keep revenue growth elevated
Forecast 27% YoY revenue growth in FY27 for Paytm (vs 22% in FY26), with EBITDA more than doubling YoY

CITI On Paytm
Buy, TP raised to Rs 1560
1Q EBITDA at Rs2bn beat estimates (+16% vs Citi) on in-line revenue (+28% YoY) & Contribution Profits (55% of rev), largely owing to lower indirect costs (cloud) and higher loan distribution (merchant) which offset lower net payment margins (device rentals)
Raise estimates
Media reports suggest MDR on high-ticket/large-merchant UPI may be introduced: 5-7bps MDR for industry may translate to 0.5-1bps upside to net payment margin & 8-10% upside to FY28E EBITDA est.

CLSA on Paytm
U-P, TP Rs 1050
1QFY27 Ebitda of Rs2bn was marginally ahead of our Rs1.9bn estimate
Acceleration in payments GMV growth to 31% YoY from mid-20s in preceding quarter stood out
Financial services (FS) revenue growth also accelerated from a high-30s percentage to a mid-40s percentage in quarter
However, calculated net take rate in payments moderated from 8.8bps to 8.3bps sequentially.
With stable contribution margin at 55%, lower fixed costs growth led to Ebitda improving from Rs1.3bn to Rs2bn sequentially.
Opex also benefitted from lower provision on doubtful debt this quarter.
Its subsidiary, Paytm Payment Services, has applied for a wallet license
Trim FY27-29 Ebitda estimates by 2-3% due to higher opex.
Recent run-up in stock price in anticipation of MDR on UPI returning leaves no upside, even if it happens
@CNBCTV18Live @_RituSingh

items.title: US corporations have never had more foreign exposure: The proportion of S&P 500 revenue generated abroad is up to a record ~42%. This surpasses the pr...
items.description_html:
US corporations have never had more foreign exposure:

The proportion of S&P 500 revenue generated abroad is up to a record ~42%.

This surpasses the previous peak of ~40% in 2015 and ~39% posted during the 2000 Dot-Com Bubble.

This comes despite the unprecedented surge in domestic AI investment, which was expected to make corporate revenue more concentrated in the US.

Technology companies are behind most of the surge, with foreign markets now accounting for over 50% of the sector's revenue.

Furthermore, the communication services sector generates ~47% of its revenue abroad, up from less than 40% before the 2020 pandemic.

The global economy is more connected than ever.

items.title: RT Ananth Rupanagudi: A letter from 5, Canning Lane, New Delhi, the residence of Pandit Ravi Shankar Shukla, the then Chief Minister of Central Provin...
items.description_html:
RT Ananth Rupanagudi
A letter from 5, Canning Lane, New Delhi, the residence of Pandit Ravi Shankar Shukla, the then Chief Minister of Central Provinces and Berar (Canning lane is now renamed as Pandit Ravi Shankar Shukla Lane) to the Home Minister in the Interim Cabinet, Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel, recommending the return of Dr. Pandurang Sadashiv Khankhoje to India.

There was some support for Dr Khankhoje to return to India from Mexico. His application for visa was initially rejected but was subsequently overturned and he returned to India after Independence in 1947. #archives #Khankhoje

items.title: The South Korean and US AI trades are becoming increasingly synchronized: The 60-day correlation between the KOSPI and Nasdaq 100 index is up to +0.46...
items.description_html:
The South Korean and US AI trades are becoming increasingly synchronized:

The 60-day correlation between the KOSPI and Nasdaq 100 index is up to +0.46, the highest since July 2024.

This marks a sharp reversal from a negative correlation of -0.20 recorded in March.

By comparison, the 5-year average is just +0.16 or nearly one-third the 60-day metric.

Furthermore, the Nasdaq 100's sensitivity to declines in South Korean stocks is up to its highest level since 1990.

At the same time, the MSCI World Index's sensitivity to the South Korean market is up to its highest in 4 years.

In other words, declines in South Korean equities are increasingly serving as an early read on US and global equity risk appetite, particularly in technology stocks.

The AI trade has never been more global.

items.title: Stress in one of the credit market's riskiest areas is intensifying: Collateralized loan obligation (CLO) equity tranches returned -15% in Q1 2026, th...
items.description_html:
Stress in one of the credit market's riskiest areas is intensifying:

Collateralized loan obligation (CLO) equity tranches returned -15% in Q1 2026, their worst quarterly performance since the 2020 pandemic crash.

These tranches represent the highest-risk layer of the $1.3 trillion CLO market, with equity investors taking the first losses when underlying loans underperform.

This also marks the 2nd consecutive quarterly loss and even exceeds the Q2 2022 bear market drop of -12%.

The selloff has been driven by a decline in software loan prices combined with fewer new corporate loans being issued.

This leaves CLO managers, the firms responsible for selecting the loans inside CLO portfolios, with fewer opportunities to invest in attractive assets, putting further pressure on returns.

Credit market stress is rising beneath the surface.

items.title: The AI boom is creating a generational divide in software jobs: US software developer employment for those aged 22-25 has declined -23% since November...
items.description_html:
The AI boom is creating a generational divide in software jobs:

US software developer employment for those aged 22-25 has declined -23% since November 2022, when ChatGPT was launched.

At the same time, the 26-30 age group has seen a decrease of -5%.

By comparison, employment among software developers aged 41-49 has risen +18% over the same period.

This creates a 41 percentage point gap between the youngest and one of the most experienced groups within the same profession.

Meanwhile, early-career workers in the most AI-exposed jobs have experienced a -16% decline in employment relative to less-exposed occupations and more experienced peers since November 2022, even after adjusting for firm-specific shocks.

By contrast, employment among workers in less AI-exposed fields, as well as more experienced workers within the same occupations, has remained steady or continued to grow.

The AI revolution is wiping out entry-level tech jobs.

items.title: Leverage is rapidly unwinding across Chinese stocks: Margin debt on the Shanghai and Shenzhen exchanges fell -2.8% on Friday, or -$11.7 billion, to $4...
items.description_html:
Leverage is rapidly unwinding across Chinese stocks:

Margin debt on the Shanghai and Shenzhen exchanges fell -2.8% on Friday, or -$11.7 billion, to $405 billion, the largest daily decline since January 2016.

This also marks the 4th consecutive daily decrease, totaling -$36.9 billion.

This comes as the Star 50 Index, which tracks Chinese technology stocks, plunged -7.1% on Friday, its 2nd-largest daily drop this year, while the CSI 300 fell -3.6%.

Memory chip stocks were at the center of the selloff after attracting the highest levels of margin borrowing.

As a result, their sharp selloff triggered margin calls, forcing investors to sell and accelerating the broader market decline.

Chip stocks have become a global amplifier of market volatility.

items.title: US stocks have almost never moved this independently of each other: The S&P 500's 6-month implied correlation is down to 0.15, an all-time low. Implie...
items.description_html:
US stocks have almost never moved this independently of each other:

The S&P 500's 6-month implied correlation is down to 0.15, an all-time low.

Implied correlation measures how closely stocks are expected to move together, with a reading this low meaning individual stocks are becoming increasingly disconnected from the broader index.

This metric has now more than HALVED over the last 4 months, when correlation was at 0.35 during the market selloff.

By comparison, the long-term average stands at 0.43, nearly 3 times the current level, while the high over this period was 0.82 during the 2020 pandemic crash.

This historic divergence highlights a market increasingly driven by a handful of mega-cap stocks, while the rest of the market trades more on its own fundamentals.

Market concentration has rarely been this extreme.

items.title: RT Ilan Goldenberg: I’ve worked on Iran for nearly 20 years. Our options are never great, but I’ve never seen things this bad. For the first time in...
items.description_html:
RT Ilan Goldenberg
I’ve worked on Iran for nearly 20 years. Our options are never great, but I’ve never seen things this bad. For the first time in my career, I’m at a loss.

When this war began, I warned that Iran would likely emerge weakened but more aggressive and risk-tolerant—leaving the region trapped in a cycle of military clashes and chronic instability. That has happened. What I underestimated was the leverage Iran would gain through its stranglehold over the Strait of Hormuz.

For a moment, the MOU offered a possible way out. It was deeply flawed, but better than the alternatives. Then vague terms and shoddy negotiating caused it to collapse: Iran believed it could retain some control over the Strait; Washington believed the Strait would reopen to international shipping.

But the fundamental problem wasn’t the negotiation. It was Trump’s catastrophic decision to go to war in the first place.

The result: higher oil prices, economic shocks, depleted US missile-defense stocks, immense regional damage and civilian suffering, alienated allies and very little achieved in return.

So what now?

We could pay Iran off through sanctions relief, oil exports and access to the international banking system. But after surviving forty days of war, Tehran is angry, distrustful and convinced it holds the stronger hand. Its price is so high – and so humiliating – that Trump will never accept it.

We could escalate militarily, which is what the US is doing. But bombing has not produced meaningful Iranian concessions, and Trump appears unwilling to bear the costs of the maximalist alternatives. More pressure means an expensive, dangerous and potentially endless war, with serious risks to US forces and the entire region as we tragically saw this weekend. And it has little chance of success.

Or we could encourage the Gulf states to negotiate a regional arrangement. That may ultimately be the most promising path, but the Gulf states are divided, this remains fundamentally a US-Iran conflict, and any agreement will require major American concessions while leaving Iran strategically stronger than it was before the war. So this isn’t very appealing and hard to get to as well.

The likeliest outcome is therefore that we are looking at a new normal: recurring US-Iran clashes, periodic American or Israeli strikes on Iran’s nuclear program, Iranian control over the SOH, higher oil prices and a larger, longer-lasting American military presence in the Middle East. But since the costs of all out escalation are too high both sides try to keep it from going there, though there is always a risk.

Iran’s leverage will eventually depreciate as countries build pipelines, diversify energy supplies and establish alternative trade routes. But that could take years.

Eventually, exhaustion may produce a deal. Iran may decide that controlling the Strait while living under blockade is unsustainable. Washington may accept that it must offer more. The Gulf states may finally unite around a workable diplomatic framework. But not soon.

Trump took a situation in which better options plainly existed, started an unnecessary war and produced a strategic disaster that will take years to unwind.
For now, we are all stuck with this mess.

items.title: RT Parimal: If you walk into the Secretariat of Public Education in Mexico City & look up at the massive, world-famous frescoes painted by Diego River...
items.description_html:
RT Parimal
If you walk into the Secretariat of Public Education in Mexico City & look up at the massive, world-famous frescoes painted by Diego Rivera, you will see something that should make every Indian’s jaw drop.

Among the painted Mexican revolutionaries, farmers & national heroes sits an Indian man with intense eyes, sitting at a long wooden table, breaking bread with poor peasants.

He is not a diplomat. He is not a movie star. He was a wanted fugitive from Maharashtra whom the British Empire spent decades trying to hunt down & the man who quietly revolutionized global agriculture before the world even knew what the "Green Revolution" was.

His name was Pandurang Sadashiv Khankhoje.

Born in 1884 in Wardha, Maharashtra, Khankhoje grew up on stories of the 1857 uprising. While other kids played games, young Pandurang spent his teens founding secret societies & training in martial arts. Inspired by Lokmanya Tilak, he reached a radical conclusion early in life: To defeat an empire, you need military training & global allies.

In 1906, penniless & under British surveillance, he fled India on a ship. He worked his way through Japan & San Francisco, eventually enrolling at the Mount Tamalpais Military Academy in California while earning a degree in agriculture at Oregon State University.

In Oregon, he co-founded the Ghadar Party, the legendary international movement aimed at overthrowing British rule in India through armed revolt.

During World War I, Khankhoje traveled across Europe & the Middle East, attempting to recruit Indian soldiers in the British Indian Army to turn their guns against their colonizers. But the revolt failed. British intelligence put a massive price on his head. He could not go back to India. He could not stay in the West. He was a man w/o a country.

Exiled & on the run, Khankhoje slipped across the border into Mexico in the 1920s. Mexico was rebuilding after its own bloody revolution & its rural population was dying of starvation & drought.

Khankhoje realized something profound: An empire does not just conquer with guns; it conquers through hunger. If you want a nation to be truly free, you have to feed its people.

Appointed as a prof at the National School of Agriculture in Texcoco, he threw himself into plant genetics. Working under harsh conditions with limited funding, he did something extraordinary:

- Taming the Wild Corn: He crossbred wild grass (teosinte) with domestic maize, creating high-yielding, drought-resistant hybrid corn that produced multiple ears per plant instead of just 1.

- Frost-Proof Wheat: He developed new strains of high-yield wheat capable of surviving extreme Mexican weather & blights.

- Schools for the Poor: He did not lock his research in academic journals. He founded the Escuelas Libres de Agricultura (Free Agricultural Schools), teaching impoverished Mexican farmers hands-on genetics, soil conservation & yield optimization.

His high-yield crops transformed Mexican farming. Decades later, when American scientist Norman Borlaug came to Mexico & developed the high-yield dwarf wheat that kicked off the global Green Revolution, he built directly upon the foundation of plant genetics laid by Pandurang Khankhoje.

In 1947, India finally won its independence. The British were gone.

Now an elderly scientist globally celebrated across Latin America, Pandurang Khankhoje packed his bags & came home to Nagpur, eager to offer his life's work to build the agricultural backbone of a newborn India.

The bureaucracy of newly independent India did not know what to do with him. His radical revolutionary past frightened bureaucrats; his foreign academic achievements were ignored. He was given no major agricultural policy role, no national honors & no platform :((

When he passed away in Nagpur in 1967, his passing barely made a ripple in the local newspapers. His neighbors knew him simply as a quiet, retired old man who used to live abroad.

Today, if you ask an Indian agricultural student who fathered modern crop hybridization, they will quote Western scientists. Almost no 1 in Maharashtra/Delhi knows the name Pandurang Khankhoje.

But 1000s of KMs away in Mexico City, inside the halls of govt, his face remains painted on the wall, immortalized by Diego Rivera as a symbol of unity, knowledge, and freedom.

He was erased from the history books of the country he fought to liberate, but the soil of Mexico still remembers the Indian ghost who taught them how to feed a nation.

items.title: Markets rarely suffer from too little information. They suffer from too little independent evidence. Consensus isn't dangerous because people agree. I...
items.description_html:
Markets rarely suffer from too little information. They suffer from too little independent evidence.

Consensus isn't dangerous because people agree.

It's dangerous because they arrive there through the same evidence.

Everyone reads the same annual reports.

Everyone listens to the same earnings calls.

Everyone reads the same broker notes. The same HBR ...

The best investments I've made didn't come from finding secret information.

They came from looking for expensive evidence.

Building capacity before revenues arrive.

Hiring ahead of demand.

Capital allocation during downturns instead of promises during booms.

Customers committing long before the market notices.

Shared information creates shared narratives.

Independent evidence creates conviction!

items.description_html:
BREAKING: Alphabet, $GOOGL, is planning on launching a new “frozen” chip to run its AI models more efficiently, per The Information.

Details include:

1. This new server chip would directly integrate the blueprint of its Gemini AI model

2. The chip would then enable Alphabet to provide its AI models to users much more efficiently

3. The chip's developers estimate that it would be 6 to 10 times more efficient than the most recent version of Google's existing line of AI chips

Another tech giant has entered the chip race.

items.title: RT ValueEquity: Wockhardt , reading this annual report today , or you can call it Antibiotics 101 Very interesting annual report if you like to read a...
items.description_html:
RT ValueEquity
Wockhardt , reading this annual report today , or you can call it Antibiotics 101

Very interesting annual report if you like to read about the pharma sector , I think one thing we have to say , irrespective of the tough times they faced in the Past , Zaynich USFDA approval is a huge success for them , and for the Indian Pharma , such an innovation in India is truly commendable

FY27 will not have much contribution , FY28-29 will have some real gains here for them in terms of volumes on such a useful drug , FY27 will see mostly increase in costs first and then FY28 will be monetization, they are and going themselves for selling in US rather than licensing

Key Pipeline
1) WCK 4282 (FoviscuTM)
2) WCK 4873 (Miqnaf®):
3) WCK 6777 (OdrateTM):
4) WCK 771 & WCK 2349 (Emrok® & Emrok O®)

Wockhardt 2.0 , 24-30 months runway ( this is a long game )

1) 12-18 months is breakeven /small profit , and after that topline is bottom line ( shared by management in NDTV profit interview )

2) Peak is 1.5B$ , peak is expected to have FY30-31 , trajectory acceleration, this is a long game , profits will only really show in FY28-29

Then just to see the approval document , I went to the FDA site to see how prescription of this is , Zaynich is used every 8 hours ( 3 times a day ) on the 3g medication

Prices in India will be discounted in India up to 75-80% from the US pricing

They are focusing on building a global innovator type company , post ramp up costs ,margins will be 10% higher at least at company level

Now the question is how do you play this , I think this is something crucial, I want better prices , just incase if we get a major correction in the ramp up phase due to short term cost impacts , would be very interesting to evaluate or then when the ramp up starts and we see these numbers and some early signs

But definitely an interesting company to keep tracking !

disc: this is not investment advice in any form , no financial reco , please do your own due diligence

items.title: RT Timsy Jaipuria: India has cleared its first-ever dengue vaccine. Drug Controller General of India (DCGI) has approved Takeda's QDENGA, making it th...
items.description_html:
RT Timsy Jaipuria
India has cleared its first-ever dengue vaccine.

Drug Controller General of India (DCGI) has approved Takeda's QDENGA, making it the country's first authorised vaccine for dengue prevention.

Key details:
• Approved for individuals aged 4–60 years
• Protects against all four dengue virus serotypes
• Can be given regardless of prior dengue infection, eliminating the need for pre-vaccination screening
• Administered as two doses, three months apart

The approval is backed by Takeda's global clinical programme involving over 28,000 participants. Clinical data showed:
✅ 80.2% efficacy against confirmed dengue one year after the second dose
✅ 90.4% efficacy against dengue-related hospitalisation after 18 months

QDENGA is already approved in 43 countries and has been distributed in more than 32 million doses globally. Experts say the vaccine could significantly reduce severe disease and hospitalisations, although mosquito control and public health measures will remain essential to curb outbreaks.

The approval marks a major milestone in India's fight against a disease that affects hundreds of thousands of people every year.

items.title: UltraTech Cement | 1QFY27 Concall Takeaways @UltraTechCement @CNBCTV18Live 1️⃣ Demand outlook remains strong; management continues to see robust dem...
items.description_html:
UltraTech Cement | 1QFY27 Concall Takeaways

@UltraTechCement @CNBCTV18Live

1️⃣ Demand outlook remains strong; management continues to see robust demand as the key investment thesis for the cement sector and is targeting double-digit volume growth in FY27

2️⃣ Cement pricing stayed constructive in Q1, with all-India exit prices improving through June. Prices are expected to remain broadly stable during the monsoon despite elevated industry cost pressures

3️⃣ Profitability target remains intact: ₹1,400/t EBITDA by exit-FY28, assuming no further global disruptions.

4️⃣ Costs are expected to rise ₹130–140/t QoQ, but management plans mitigation measures to absorb the impact

5️⃣ Successfully completed 100% brand conversion of India Cements and Kesoram

6️⃣ For India Cements, management reiterated its ₹1,000/t EBITDA target, with the full benefit of operational improvement capex expected by Q4FY28

7️⃣ Wires & Cables business remains on track for launch in early 3QFY27

items.title: AI is accelerating US business formation: The number of businesses in AI-related sectors, including professional, scientific, and technical services, ...
items.description_html:
AI is accelerating US business formation:

The number of businesses in AI-related sectors, including professional, scientific, and technical services, has grown +45% since the launch of ChatGPT in November 2022.

By comparison, the number of construction businesses has increased +10% over the same period.

Furthermore, business creation has risen +20% across the broader US economy.

The Census Bureau now projects +29,700 new businesses will form monthly nationwide over the next year, up +17% YoY.

Professional services are expected to account for more than 5,000 new businesses per month, a record for the sector and up +24% YoY.

AI is fueling a new wave of entrepreneurship.

In May 2021, Solar Industries also participated in Skyroot's Series A funding round alongside Greenko promoters and other investors.

Today the stake is worth ~Rs 1000 cr!

Beyond being an investor, Solar Industries became a strategic partner, supplying solid rocket propulsion systems and supporting static testing. Skyroot's successful Vikram-1 mission further highlighted this partnership

I love it when bigger companies help startups with Capital and know-how!

Competition is good, but Collaboration is key!

items.title: Last September, #Sansera was a premium-only note. Today, I'm making it free for everyone! I'm still holding it with both hands. My risk-reward equatio...
items.description_html:
Last September, #Sansera was a premium-only note.

Today, I'm making it free for everyone!

I'm still holding it with both hands. My risk-reward equation and my ability to absorb sharp drawdowns are very different from yours, and the stock has also been 2.5x since. There is no recommendation to make it very explicit.

The thesis was never about the next quarter. It was about recognising a precision engineering business evolving into a high-value #CDMO across aerospace, defence, semiconductors and industrials before the market fully appreciated it.

I hope the framework remains useful. Good mental models often outlast a single investment.

Free to read below.

https://antifragilethinking.substack.com/p/framing-business-uncertainty-semiconductors

Piran argues that markets are overly focused on NIM compression in Q1. Says this largely due to corp loan books growing quickly. While this hits NIMs, these loans typically come with lower opex & lower credit costs.

Piran also lays out this interesting statistic-> "banking sector has not hired new employees in the last two years (18 lk employees has not changed).. yet there has been biz growth... resulting in significant improvements in the opex-to-assets ratio across banks.."

#Nifty #BankNifty #India #Banking

items.title: RT Naga Bharath Daka: After years of staying mostly silent here, I wanted to share what this moment truly means for us. Reaching orbit with Vikram-1 i...
items.description_html:
RT Naga Bharath Daka
After years of staying mostly silent here, I wanted to share what this moment truly means for us.

Reaching orbit with Vikram-1 is the culmination of an incredibly long and difficult journey. Over the last few years, Team Skyroot pushed relentlessly through missed aggressive deadlines, technical hurdles, and moments when the finish line kept moving. We never lost hope. Not once.

This success feels like redemption for every late night, every difficult choice, and every person who refused to give up on the dream.

The flight has given us a treasure of high-fidelity data. We will use every bit of it to make our vehicle subsystems significantly more robust and to optimize performance across the entire system.

For any launch company, achieving orbit is the single hardest milestone. Productionizing this capability, turning this success into reliable, repeatable flights, is now our central focus.

To every member of Team Skyroot who carried this dream when it was hardest, and to everyone who stood with us through the quiet years: thank you. This is your victory too.

To the entire nation that has celebrated this achievement with so much warmth and pride, thank you! It has meant more to us than words can express.

The real work begins now. 🚀

@SkyrootA

items.title: Baazar Style has disclosed two civil proceedings against Cupid ltd / related parties This is interesting because Cupid had recently come in as a strat...
items.description_html:
Baazar Style has disclosed two civil proceedings against Cupid ltd / related parties

This is interesting because Cupid had recently come in as a strategic investor in Baazar Style through preferential warrants

Cupid subscribed to 1.01 Cr equity warrants at 328.25 per warrant, aggregating to 331.53 Cr. Baazar Style had received 25% upfront amount of 82.88 Cr from Cupid

And now within a few months, Baazar Style has filed two civil suits against Cupid

The company has claimed a decree of 28.18 Cr along with other consequential reliefs

So basically, Cupid first invests via preferential allotment, and now Baazar Style is taking legal action against Cupid / related parties

What the heck is happening here?

When a strategic investor enters through a large preferential issue & soon after becomes part of litigation with the company, it raises a lot of questions

They also stopped doing concalls which is a big red flag

items.title: "From my student days, I have been interested in the equity market. I used to read some balance sheets because my father had some shares, and he used ...
items.description_html:
"From my student days, I have been interested in the equity market. I used to read some balance sheets because my father had some shares, and he used to get balance sheets at home. My first investment began when I was a student. I applied for a few IPOs, and I remember I got allotted in a company called Godrej Gujarat Innovative or something. From investing a thousand rupees, I made ten thousand rupees at that point of time, which was quite big.

Obviously, money is something that lures you. After I completed my CA, I used to do small bits of investment, read balance sheets, and do limited research because information at that time was not as easily available as it is today. Harshad Mehta, obviously, was a big factor that catapulted the market at that point of time.

Also, during my practicing days, a few of my clients were stock brokers on the Bombay Stock Exchange. At that time, just visiting the BSE building used to be so fascinating. So, it was a combination of a lot of these things. And when NSE started operations in 1994, that is the time when I got an opportunity. Maybe it was destined, but I took on that opportunity."

- Sunil Singhania Sir in a MoneyControl Article (citing his interview with Marcellus), no stock reco

items.title: ACCIDENT OF THE DAY : WAAREE, PREMIER ENERGIES *ALMM-II extension, -ve for Solar mfg, +ve for developers* *What’s new?* - MNRE, on Saturday, announce...
items.description_html:
ACCIDENT OF THE DAY : WAAREE, PREMIER ENERGIES

*ALMM-II extension, -ve for Solar mfg, +ve for developers*

*What’s new?*
- MNRE, on Saturday, announced limited extension to the implementation of ALMM List-II (Approved List of Models & Manufacturers for solar cells) for Net Metering and Open Access (OA) solar projects to Dec-26 (earlier: May-26).
- The decision followed by industry concerns that domestic cell manufacturing capacity remains insufficient to meet near-term demand, potentially delaying projects and increasing costs.
- MNRE stated that the move is intended to facilitate a smoother transition towards domestic cell sourcing while protecting investments already made by developers and module manufacturers.
- Interestingly, the order specifically notes that the extension will also help module manufacturers monetize existing inventories and provide time for the domestic solar cell industry to scale up capacity before the full implementation of ALMM List-II.

*Our view*

1) Impact on Solar Module Manufacturers (Waaree Energies & Premier Energies)
Near-term impact: Negative
- This extension reduces the value creation opportunity for the manufacturers such as Waaree Energies and Premier Energies with stronger backward intergration
- As the industry manufacturing capacity expands and demand supply mismatch reduces over the next six months, the pricing power for early movers of cell capacity additon would erode for the DCR based modules
- On the non DCR module, there can be an increase in demand in Q2 and Q3, however, given the lower realization and margin compared to DCR modules, this would still be net negative on the FY27 financials of these companies.

2) *Impact on CleanMax*
*Near term impact: Positive*
- For RE open access and rooftop developers like CleanMax, this could have a significant boost in the segment as there can be an increased demand from consumer to benefit from the capex cost savings
- For C&I developer, execution certainty is often more valuable than marginal equipment localization benefits, making this policy amendment an incremental positive for growth and project returns over the next six months.

*3) Industry impact*
- In our view, the strong solar capacity addition in FY26 was partly driven by capacity additions ramp up to benefit prior to the change in regulations.
- We earlier, therefore had anticipated the spurt of FY26 (44.6GW addition) to subside marginally in FY27 and estimated a 35GW addition for the year.
- However, the announced extension, we see upside risk to our estimate. BROKERAGE NOTE

items.title: Everyone wants US stocks. Foreign private sector net purchases of US stocks rose +$35.2 billion MoM in May, to $121 billion, the 2nd-largest inflow on...
items.description_html:
Everyone wants US stocks.

Foreign private sector net purchases of US stocks rose +$35.2 billion MoM in May, to $121 billion, the 2nd-largest inflow on record.

This marks the 2nd consecutive monthly increase.

By comparison, the record monthly inflow was ~$130 billion in November 2024.

Year-to-date, foreign private investors have purchased ~$270 billion of US stocks.

To put this into perspective, foreign investors dumped -$31 billion and -$28 billion of South Korean stocks in June and May, respectively, the two largest monthly outflows on record.

Furthermore, foreign investors also sold -$18 billion of Taiwanese stocks in June, the 2nd-largest sale on record.

Global capital continues to favor US equities over the rest of the world.

items.title: RT Aaron Levie: A lot of people make the mistake of thinking that when AI costs drop, that spend on AI drops with it. Usually the opposite happens. Wh...
items.description_html:
RT Aaron Levie
A lot of people make the mistake of thinking that when AI costs drop, that spend on AI drops with it. Usually the opposite happens.

When you make AI cheaper, it will get consumed more. Because now you can afford to use for a wider of tasks than you did before. You start to write more code. You review more code for bugs and security. You run agents on large data sets you couldn’t process before. And so on.

Thus, for the foreseeable future, anything that lowers the cost of tokens will drive up inference demand.

This also gives you some insight also into why even open source business models work in AI. No one is running these models on their devices; they’re running them in infra. Great time to be one of those providers.

items.description_html:
RT Surya Kanegaonkar
Launch demand will only grow with time for military, weather, communication, data center, mapping, asteroid mining and scientific applications. Over a twenty year time horizon, the domestic market will probably match supply. Add on top demand from EMs and middle powers that share strong diplomatic and security relationships with the country, and you’re looking at capacity saturation in all likelihood.

The real bottleneck will not be the tech - that will be cracked - but rather the components. Indigenizing the supply chain for both launch vehicles and satellites will be critical for keeping the industry safe from foreign export controls. Ultimately, the ability to scale depends on funding, and funding increasingly depends on production and launch guarantees that hinge on security of component supply.

items.title: My two-cents on The Odyssey: I came away underwhelmed. Visually, it is spectacular, with some incredible set pieces. The most impressive are Circe tur...
items.description_html:
My two-cents on The Odyssey: I came away underwhelmed.

Visually, it is spectacular, with some incredible set pieces. The most impressive are Circe turning the men into pigs (I would watch a spinoff just about Circe), the Cyclops Polyphemus in his cave, Scylla striking from the rocks, and the towering Laestrygonians.

But the film has no soul. And one important part of The Odyssey is about a man searching for his soul, working out who he really is after victory and glory. It felt like Nolan seems to have absorbed too much from recent superhero movies—the ones that hand you great action, visuals, and set pieces but leave little emotional residue behind once you walk out.

What I liked: The film is visually incredible. The sound design is among the best I have experienced in one of these big-experience movies, and the production design, the cinematography, the use of IMAX film, and the absence of CGI are all impressive. The war scenes are pretty good (though I don’t know why Agamemnon is wearing a Darth Vader-esque costume). The casting, which has riled up everyone, didn’t bother me. Argos was the best casting in the movie, followed by Pattinson as Antinous.

What baffled me: The real failure is that the gods and demigods are barely there. A Greek epic without the gods talking, interfering, and conspiring makes little sense to me. This is the Hollywood version, stripped of the mysticism and uncertainty at the heart of the story. Athena is reduced to almost nothing. She shakes her head, her eyes brim with tears, and that is about all the film gives her. Hermes never appears. Calypso comes off as a hippie island girl, not a nymph deity.

Once you take the gods and goddesses out, the whole thing goes flat, because now Odysseus reads only as a poor, lost man trying to get home (insert the usual meme about Hollywood trying to bring Matt Damon home). But Odysseus is a proud man, a vain man, who thinks he controls his destiny until the gods put him in his place over and over again. In Nolan’s version, the wrath of each god turns up as another weather event. There are too many stormy scenes. I understand that it is a seafaring journey, but there is far more to it than that.

The biggest disappointment was the music, which was soulless and flat. Losing the Sirens’ song and turning it into a voice-over was a poor choice.

It is definitely worth a watch. It is not Nolan’s best movie. And to truly experience The Odyssey, read the book.

items.title: Wrote this as an X article last week. Posting it in case you missed it. AI’s Trust Problem = Advantage Indian IT Cos ? The power of AI is no longer i...
items.description_html:
Wrote this as an X article last week. Posting it in case you missed it.

AI’s Trust Problem = Advantage Indian IT Cos ?

The power of AI is no longer in question. What is increasingly being debated is the price companies may pay for using it !

Consider the recent developments,
- OpenAI & Apple
- Alex Karp’s CNBC interview
- Satya Nadella’s on “reverse information paradox”

.... all point to the same emerging concern - that AI may not simply consume corporate data.. that it may absorb the proprietary knowledge embedded within it.

Nadella framed this as a reversal of the traditional buyer-seller relationship. The buyer pays twice - first with money & then with something far more valuable - the internal knowledge required to make the AI useful.

The worry is, that over time, this exchange can become deeply asymmetric. The AI provider learns more about the customer, its biz, its processes & its IP. The customer, meanwhile, gains little visibility into what the provider (the model cos) are learning from those interactions.. or how that accumulated knowledge may eventually be used.

This does matter, because IP is not confined to patents, code or documents. It also exists as institutional knowledge.. judgment, methods transferred via mentorship, instincts etc. Historically, firms had to hire people to get all this knowledge. AI companies may now be able to acquire fragments of it through the very products they sell.

That is why Alex Karp’s argument carries weight. Companies increasingly want control over their compute, their models, their data stack & their alpha. They want to know that they still own the means of production & that their competitive advantage is not quietly being transferred elsewhere.

Could this be advantage Indian T services companies ?

Nandan Nilekani spoke about this @ the Infosys Analyst Day earlier this year. I am paraphrasing, but his message was that Indian IT services firms have been trusted inside the most sensitive systems of global corps. They understand not only the technology, but also the implicit knowledge, undocumented dependencies & operating context that make those businesses work.

In the AI era, could that trust & institutional context become their most valuable asset ? Some food for thought..

#Nifty #BankNifty #ITServicesCos #Technology

items.title: Government incentive for UPI/Rupay transactions is close to 2000cr and it takes cumulatively 8-10k cr rupees a year to sustain the UPI ecosystem in in...
items.description_html:
Government incentive for UPI/Rupay transactions is close to 2000cr and it takes cumulatively 8-10k cr rupees a year to sustain the UPI ecosystem in india. Also this digital incentive model is a bit unpredictable.

Participants are now seeking a fee for high value transaction while keeping low value transactions untouched. Just for context - transactions more than 2000 rs. account for only 4% of value but 67% of value so a fee slice on that can generate a meaningfull revenue for the participants. Even a 15bps MDR on P2M transactions can generate a revenue pool of $1 bn

Even so, the eventual gain would depend on how the economics are shared among banks, payment apps, merchant acquirers and NPCI.

The talks in the industry for MDR application is coming back but still it remains optionality for the players and players with strong base business growth and are not dependent on the UPI incentives for them to survive.

Ecosystem will need to find ways to make the economics work without expecting a subsidy or a broad MDR revival.

The biggest cost burden sits with banks, which have to build the real-time infrastructure for UPI, but can absorb it because they earn money from other parts of the business. But payment apps such as PhonePe and Google Pay get a large amount of customer engagement, and they should find a way to monetise that through ads or other products.

Net net talks of MDR coming back is again coming up but one should think of this as a optionality and if it comes can be big for all the participants including PAYTM

items.title: RT Harish Ramkumar: Re Really solid gupshup, Prashant. Anish’s framing was refreshingly clear-eyed. The “time to be a net seller of land” point sto...
items.description_html:
RT Harish Ramkumar
Re Really solid gupshup, Prashant. Anish’s framing was refreshingly clear-eyed.
The “time to be a net seller of land” point stood out — land prices have run hard and are cyclical, while actual house prices are still more reasonable. Preferring companies that are monetising land (shrinking balance sheets + dividends) over those still accumulating makes a lot of sense right now.
Equally useful was the re-labelling of “affordable housing finance” as closer to sub-prime than prime, and the reminder that headline lending penetration vs the US is misleading once you adjust for microfinance and unsecured. Two segments that look overheated.
On insurance, the distinction between insurer economics and distributor economics is important — any regulatory heat is more likely to fall on the latter.
Would love a follow-up on how he’s positioning the DSP portfolios around these views (especially financials vs pure real-estate plays). Thanks for the conversation.

items.title: With Vikram-1 successfully reaching orbit, Skyroot's biggest challenge now shifts to building a commercial launch business and scaling launch frequenc...
items.description_html:
With Vikram-1 successfully reaching orbit, Skyroot's biggest challenge now shifts to building a commercial launch business and scaling launch frequency.

Skyroot's business model will be different from companies like Elon Musk's SpaceX, though both are in the business of building rockets that can carry satellites.

Skyroot founder Pawan Chandana across multiple interviews has described the business using a simple analogy: its rockets are like taking cabs to space, while companies like SpaceX are more like metros or trains.

According to him, Skyroot plans to take customers directly to their desired orbit.

Larger launch providers such as SpaceX are optimised for commonly used orbits and rideshare missions rather than highly customised deployments.

So a customer may want satellites to pass over a region close to the equator. That requires launching into an orbit that isn't commonly served.

The argument is that they can't justify booking a $70 million SpaceX rocket for that mission.

Instead, they can book a Skyroot rocket for a lower price and reach their required orbit.

The closest listed peer for the business Skyroot today is gunning for is Nasdaq-listed Rocket Lab rather than SpaceX.

Rocket Lab, founded in 2006 and now valued at about $40 billion, also serves the small satellite market through dedicated launches.

Its Electron rocket also targets the small satellite market with dedicated launches rather than competing head-on with SpaceX's heavy-lift Falcon 9.

Electron is priced at around $8-9 million per dedicated launch, as per reports and filings.

Chandana told Mint last year that each Vikram launch would cost Skyroot $2-3 million and generate about $5 million in revenue.

I am guessing these numbers might change a bit now, but Skyroot will likely keep its launch pricing competitive with Rocket Lab's Electron.

Skyroot plans to reach a launch cadence of one mission a quarter, and then one a month as quickly as possible.

For context, Rocket Lab's Electron has completed 70 launches so far, including 21 in 2025.

If Skyroot reaches a cadence of one launch a month and maintains launch pricing around current estimates,
that could translate into roughly $60-100 million of annual launch revenue.

But launches are only one part of the opportunity.

Rocket Lab now generates about two-thirds of its revenue from satellite manufacturing, components, software, mission operations and engineering services.

Skyroot may eventually follow a similar playbook as it develops larger launch vehicles and expands its payload capabilities.

Chandana estimates that about 70-80% of Skyroot's business will come from global customers, while India will likely account for only 20-30%.

Its core customers will be satellite companies building Earth observation and communications satellites.

It also has reusable rockets on its roadmap, which Chandana believes can dramatically reduce the cost of sending payloads to space. Lower launch costs could unlock new markets such as space-based data centres, astronaut missions and space tourism.

But reusable rockets will not come cheap.

Rocket Lab has also been developing one called Neutron to compete with SpaceX.

Its cumulative capital expenditure and development costs are estimated at around $400 million, versus an original budget of $250-300 million.

So while Skyroot is backed by the likes of Singapore's GIC and recently closed a $60 million round, it will likely need to raise significantly more capital as well to develop this.

So while Rocket Lab offers the closest public market comparison today, Skyroot's trajectory could also be very different few years down the line.

The commercial space market is maturing rapidly - Skyroot took 8 years while Rocket Lab took 12 years for their first orbital launch success.

Opportunity available to new entrants could be very different.

items.title: 🚨 C2C’s auditor has issued a qualified opinion and flagged multiple serious financial and compliance concerns: 1️⃣ Liquidity & Going Concern Ris...
items.description_html:
🚨 C2C’s auditor has issued a qualified opinion and flagged multiple serious financial and compliance concerns:

1️⃣ Liquidity & Going Concern Risk:
The company is facing a liquidity crisis, with statutory dues unpaid due to lack of funds. Recovery from trade receivables remains uncertain, while operations are heavily dependent on short-term promoter funding. The auditor has raised doubt over C2C’s ability to continue as a going concern. Liquidation Risk

2️⃣ CSR Payments Not Cleared:
CSR expenses were recognised through cheques, creating a book overdraft. However, the cheques were not presented for payment. The auditor noted that the CSR obligation is discharged only when the payment is actually cleared.

3️⃣ Statutory Dues Pending:
Undisputed statutory payments remain unpaid. No provision has been made for possible interest or penalties. Delays in filing statutory returns and weak internal controls were also highlighted.

4️⃣ Labour Code Impact Not Assessed:
The company has not quantified the potential impact of new labour regulations on gratuity, provident fund, wages and other employee benefits.

5️⃣ FEMA Compliance Issues:
Overseas receivables remain unrealised beyond RBI timelines. Applications for regularisation are pending, SOFTEX registration is yet to be approved, and possible penalties have not been provided for.

items.description_html:
RT Trung Phan
While playing at FC Barcelona, Messi became a Spanish citizen in 2005 and Spanish National Team tried to recruit him.

Argentine Team didn’t know Messi
well but he wanted to play for his home country.

So, Messi’s dad made highlight tape and sent it around. Eventually, someone from Team Argentina saw it and tried tracking down Messi’s family by lookign up “Messi” in the phone book for residents of Rosario, Argentina.

Called them one-by-one until they got a hold of Messi’s grandmother and the rest is history.


Trung Phan: Kobe telling the story (in Spanish) of when Ronaldinho visited LA with FC Barcelona in the mid-2000s.

Ronaldinho told him, “I'm going to introduce you to the guy who is going to be the greatest player of all times.”

It was a 17-year old Messi.

Re Here's the corrected version of your post: This is such an underrated tailwind for India's economic future. Young women now account for nearly half of new professionals in medical and CA fields with 58% of NEET 2026 qualifiers and 48% of new CAs in 2025. An increase in women's workforce participation will be the biggest driver of an increase in India's per capita income and key to sustaining real 8% GDP growth. items.title: *ICICI Bank – Q1FY27 FC: Firing on all cylinders* # ICICI Bank reported a 10% beat on PAT/RoA at 2.5% at INR148bn driven by healthy NII growth, and h...
items.description_html:
*ICICI Bank – Q1FY27 FC: Firing on all cylinders*

# ICICI Bank reported a 10% beat on PAT/RoA at 2.5% at INR148bn driven by healthy NII growth, and higher other income, partly offset by higher staff expenses and provisions.

# Credit growth was robust and system beating at 19.6% YoY/ 5% QoQ, mainly driven by corporate, rural and business banking, but retail growth remains relatively slow at 12% yoy.

# NIM improved 4bps QoQ to 4.36% in Q1 while the peers have reported decline in margins. This could be partly due to better int income on RBI balances and lower cash on book.

# Asset quality remained largely stable with GNPA ratio declining 2bps QoQ at 1.38%. Gross slippages stood lower at INR55.5bn in Q1FY27 versus our expectations of INR60.4bn.

# The bank holds contingency provision of INR131bn, and additional standard asset provision of INR12.8bn made in Q3 2026 as directed by RBI in respect of the agricultural priority sector portfolio.

ICICI has posted an all around strong performance delivering robust growth, margins and profitability. Broker Note
